target.title: fix: propagate gateway TLS fingerprints to bootstrap clients
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: Gateway clients bootstrapped outside `callGateway` (ACP, MCP channel bridge, and native approval handling) did not receive the local TLS certificate fingerprint for a self-signed local WSS gateway.
- Why it matters: those clients could fail TLS verification even when the local gateway bootstrap had enough information to trust the generated certificate.
- What changed: added a shared TLS fingerprint resolver, reused it from `callGateway`, propagated `bootstrap.tlsFingerprint` into ACP/MCP/approval `GatewayClient` construction, and added regression coverage for local, remote, override, and non-TLS cases.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): no gateway server TLS behavior, auth mode, config schema, environment variable, or public endpoint changed.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [x] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [x] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [x] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes: N/A
- Related: N/A
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior or issue addressed: ACP bootstrap can reach a locally configured TLS gateway using the bootstrapped local TLS fingerprint instead of failing certificate verification.
- Real environment tested: local OpenClaw checkout on Linux, temporary config/state under `/tmp/openclaw-tls-pr-smoke`, self-signed local WSS gateway on `127.0.0.1:19791`, auth disabled for the smoke.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:

```bash
OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH=/tmp/openclaw-tls-pr-smoke/openclaw.json \
OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR=/tmp/openclaw-tls-pr-smoke/state \
pnpm openclaw gateway run --allow-unconfigured --port 19791 --ws-log compact

OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH=/tmp/openclaw-tls-pr-smoke/openclaw.json \
OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR=/tmp/openclaw-tls-pr-smoke/state \
pnpm openclaw gateway health --url wss://127.0.0.1:19791

printf 'exit\n' | \
OPENCLAW_CONFIG_PATH=/tmp/openclaw-tls-pr-smoke/openclaw.json \
OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR=/tmp/openclaw-tls-pr-smoke/state \
pnpm openclaw acp client --cwd /home/jet/openclaw-dev
```

- Evidence after fix (copied terminal/log output):

```text
Gateway Health
OK (27ms)

[gateway] http server listening ...
[gateway] ready
[ws] ⇄ res ✓ health ...

ACP output reached the gateway connection path and then stopped at the expected local scope approval gate:
gateway connect failed: GatewayClientRequestError: scope upgrade pending approval (requestId: 84eb4a78-9285-4ccf-8ee1-8ba98c5945a5)
ACP bridge failed: scope upgrade pending approval (requestId: 84eb4a78-9285-4ccf-8ee1-8ba98c5945a5)
```

- Observed result after fix: local TLS health succeeded, and ACP reached the gateway authorization path without a TLS/self-signed certificate or fingerprint mismatch.
- What was not tested: a full ACP prompt turn after approving the scope upgrade in the temporary local gateway.
- Before evidence (optional but encouraged): the bug was covered by the new regression tests rather than a saved failing runtime log.

## Root Cause (if applicable)

- Root cause: local TLS fingerprint selection lived inside `callGateway`, while shared bootstrap resolution only returned URL/auth/timeout data. ACP, MCP channel bridge, and operator approval clients constructed `GatewayClient` without the local TLS fingerprint.
- Missing detection / guardrail: no test asserted that bootstrap clients forward a resolved TLS fingerprint into `GatewayClient`.
- Contributing context (if known): remote-mode and environment URL overrides needed explicit precedence rules so remote pins are applied only to TLS remote targets.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [x] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file: `src/gateway/client-bootstrap.test.ts`, `src/acp/server.startup.test.ts`, `src/gateway/operator-approvals-client.test.ts`.
- Scenario the test should lock in: local TLS bootstrap returns and forwards the local fingerprint; explicit CLI fingerprints win; remote TLS fingerprints are inherited only for TLS remote targets; non-TLS targets do not receive TLS pins.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: it validates the shared resolver and each client construction seam without needing a long-lived gateway process.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): `src/gateway/call.test.ts` covered `callGateway` behavior only.
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

Local self-signed TLS gateway clients launched through ACP, MCP channel bridge, and native approval handling now inherit the same trusted fingerprint behavior that `callGateway` already had.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
resolve bootstrap -> GatewayClient(url, token) -> local WSS cert verification can fail

After:
resolve bootstrap -> resolve TLS fingerprint -> GatewayClient(url, token, tlsFingerprint) -> local WSS trust succeeds
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`): No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`): No
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`): No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`): No
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`): No
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: N/A

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: Linux local development machine
- Runtime/container: Node/pnpm local checkout
- Model/provider: N/A
- Integration/channel (if any): Gateway local WSS, ACP client smoke
- Relevant config (redacted): temporary config with `gateway.mode=local`, `gateway.tls.enabled=true`, `gateway.tls.autoGenerate=true`, `gateway.bind=loopback`, `gateway.port=19791`

### Steps

1. Start a temporary local TLS gateway with self-signed auto-generated certs.
2. Run `pnpm openclaw gateway health --url wss://127.0.0.1:19791` against that gateway.
3. Start `pnpm openclaw acp client --cwd /home/jet/openclaw-dev` against the same temporary config.
4. Run targeted gateway/ACP/MCP regression tests and changed-file checks.
5. Run Codex review against `origin/main` and address the findings.

### Expected

- Bootstrap clients receive the correct TLS fingerprint for the selected gateway target.
- Local WSS bootstrap does not fail due to the self-signed gateway certificate.
- Remote TLS pins are not applied to plain `ws://` targets or local fallback targets.

### Actual

- `gateway health` returned `OK` over local WSS.
- ACP reached the gateway authorization path and stopped at the expected scope upgrade approval gate, with no TLS/fingerprint failure.
- Targeted tests and changed-file checks passed.
- Codex review reported no remaining actionable correctness issues after two review fixes.

## Evidence

- [ ] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [x] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

Validation commands run locally:

```bash
OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=1 pnpm test \
  src/gateway/client-bootstrap.test.ts \
  src/gateway/operator-approvals-client.test.ts \
  src/gateway/call.test.ts \
  src/acp/server.startup.test.ts \
  src/mcp/channel-server.test.ts

pnpm check:changed
git diff --check
codex review --base origin/main
```

Results:

```text
3 Vitest shards, 114 tests passed
pnpm check:changed passed
git diff --check passed
codex review --base origin/main: No actionable correctness issues were found
```

## Human Verification (required)

What you personally verified (not just CI), and how:

- Verified scenarios: local TLS fingerprint selection, explicit fingerprint precedence, remote WSS fingerprint inheritance, `ws://` remote target exclusion, remote-mode local fallback choosing local TLS fingerprint, ACP/MCP/approval client propagation seams.
- Edge cases checked: CLI override vs config-derived URL, env URL override, remote WSS, remote plain WS, missing remote URL fallback, no TLS config.
- What you did **not** verify: a full ACP prompt turn after manually approving the temporary local scope upgrade.

AI-assisted implementation note: this patch was implemented with OpenClaw/Codex assistance, and Codex review was run locally before PR creation.

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [ ] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes/No`): Yes
- Config/env changes? (`Yes/No`): No
- Migration needed? (`Yes/No`): No
- If yes, exact upgrade steps: N/A

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: applying a remote TLS fingerprint to the wrong target could break local fallback or non-TLS gateway connections.
  - Mitigation: the shared resolver only applies remote fingerprints to selected TLS remote targets, with regression tests for `ws://` and missing-remote-url fallback behavior.

target.title: fix(ios): read IDEProvisioningTeamByIdentifier
target.body:
> UPDATE: created this PR while trying out the openclaw iOS app. Everything worked out of the box except of `scripts/ios-team-id.sh`. So I thought this tiny fix might be of use. Reg. agents feedback about tests: Didn't wrote tests that execute python code since there were no existing ones that do that.

## Summary

- Problem: `scripts/ios-team-id.sh` only read `IDEProvisioningTeams` and legacy fallbacks. On my local Xcode prefs, the team list is stored under `IDEProvisioningTeamByIdentifier`, so team lookup failed.
- Why it matters: `scripts/ios-configure-signing.sh` then exits with `WARN: Unable to detect an Apple Team ID; keeping existing iOS signing override (if any).`

target.title: fix(cron): allow timeoutSeconds: 0 for no-timeout mode
target.body:
## 🐛 Problem

The cron job UI validation logic incorrectly rejects `timeoutSeconds: 0`, which should represent "no timeout" mode.

**Current behavior:**
```typescript
if (timeout <= 0) {
  errors.timeoutSeconds = "cron.errors.timeoutInvalid";
}
```
This prevents users from setting `timeoutSeconds = 0` to disable timeout entirely.

## ✅ Solution

Update validation to allow `0` as a valid value (meaning "no timeout"):

```typescript
// 0 = no timeout, > 0 = timeout in seconds
if (timeout !== 0 && timeout <= 0) {
  errors.timeoutSeconds = "cron.errors.timeoutInvalid";
}
```

## 📁 Files Changed

- `ui/src/ui/controllers/cron.ts` - Updated `validateCronForm` function

## 🧪 Testing

- Create a cron job with `timeoutSeconds: 0`
- Verify it saves without validation errors
- Confirm the job runs without timeout restrictions

## 📊 Impact

- **User-facing**: Users can now disable timeout for long-running cron jobs
- **Backward compatible**: Existing jobs with `timeoutSeconds > 0` are unaffected
- **Risk**: Low - single line change, clear semantic intent

## 📝 Related

Closes #41272

target.title: fix(cli): warn about agent-level model overrides on config set
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: `openclaw config set agents.defaults.model.primary <model>` silently succeeds without informing the user that agents with explicit `model` in `agents.list[]` will ignore the new default.
- Why it matters: users changing their default model expect all agents to switch, but agent-level overrides silently take priority. This leads to confusing behavior where `/new` still shows the old model despite the config appearing correct. In practice this caused a 1-hour debugging session chasing phantom issues (stale sessions, process restarts) before discovering the actual cause.
- What changed: after `config set` writes the config, if the path targets `agents.defaults.model` or `agents.defaults.model.primary`, the CLI now checks `agents.list[]` for agents with explicit model overrides and prints a warning listing them.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): no runtime model resolution changes, no config schema changes, no gateway restart behavior changes.

target.title: fix: stop swallowing mkdir errors in memory ensureDir
target.body:
## Summary

Retargets the stale `ensureDir()` fix to the active memory host SDK helper at `packages/memory-host-sdk/src/host/internal.ts`.

`mkdirSync({ recursive: true })` already treats an existing directory as success. Errors that still escape from it are real failures, such as permission errors, read-only filesystems, disk-full cases, or an intermediate path that is a file. Returning the directory after one of those failures makes memory indexing report the wrong problem later.

## What changed

- Remove the bare `catch {}` around `mkdirSync({ recursive: true })`.
- Add regression coverage for surfaced directory creation failures.
- Add an Unreleased changelog entry.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior or issue addressed: memory directory creation should fail fast when the host cannot create the requested directory, instead of reporting that an unusable path is ready.

Real environment tested: Windows 11 PowerShell checkout at `C:\dev\GITHUB-clean\openclaw`, Node.js 24.x, built OpenClaw output in `dist/`.

Exact steps or command run after this patch: after `corepack pnpm@10.23.0 build:strict-smoke`, imported the built `dist/internal-*.js` helper with Node and called `ensureDir()` first on a writable temp directory, then on `temp/blocker/memory` where `temp/blocker` was a regular file.

Evidence after fix: terminal output from the built helper run:

```text
writable directory created: true
mkdir error code: ENOTDIR
mkdir error surfaced: true
```

Observed result after fix: the writable directory still succeeds, and the invalid path surfaces `ENOTDIR` immediately instead of returning the unusable path.

What was not tested: full memory indexing against a live external embedding backend.

## Test plan

- [x] `corepack pnpm@10.23.0 install --frozen-lockfile`
- [x] `corepack pnpm@10.23.0 test packages/memory-host-sdk/src/host/internal.test.ts`
- [x] `corepack pnpm@10.23.0 exec oxfmt --check CHANGELOG.md packages/memory-host-sdk/src/host/internal.ts packages/memory-host-sdk/src/host/internal.test.ts`

target.title: fix(config): infer auth profile providers from profile ids
target.body:
## Summary
- fix config validation for auth profiles that rely on their profile id to imply the provider
- backfill missing `auth.profiles[*].provider` values from the `<provider>:<name>` profile key before schema validation
- add a regression test for configs that keep an OAuth profile after its provider entry was removed

## Problem
`auth.profiles` entries are identified by `<provider>:<name>`, and existing configs rely on that key to imply the provider. If the profile omits an explicit `provider` field, config validation currently fails before CLI commands can run. This shows up when a user removes a provider entry but still has an auth profile for it.

## Root Cause
`validateConfigObjectRaw()` passes raw auth profiles straight into `OpenClawSchema`, where `auth.profiles[*].provider` is still required. We already know the provider from the profile id, but we were not normalizing that value before schema validation.

## Fix
Backfill missing auth profile `provider` values from the profile id before running legacy checks and schema validation. This keeps existing profile-key semantics intact without changing configs that already set `provider`.

## Verification
- `pnpm exec vitest run src/config/config.schema-regressions.test.ts`
- `pnpm exec oxfmt --check src/config/validation.ts src/config/config.schema-regressions.test.ts`
- `pnpm exec tsx -e "import { withTempHomeConfig, withEnvOverride } from './src/config/test-helpers.ts'; import { clearConfigCache, loadConfig } from './src/config/config.ts'; (async () => { await withTempHomeConfig({ models: { providers: {} }, auth: { profiles: { 'qwen-portal:default': { mode: 'oauth' } } } }, async ({home}) => { await withEnvOverride({ HOME: home }, async () => { clearConfigCache(); const cfg = loadConfig(); console.log(JSON.stringify(cfg.auth?.profiles?.['qwen-portal:default'] ?? null)); }); }); })();"`

Fixes #38913

target.title: fix: render remote https/http images inline in Control UI chat
target.body: Fixes #40945 The `htmlEscapeRenderer` in `ui/src/ui/markdown.ts` only emitted `` tags for `data:image/...` base64 URIs. Any markdown image with a safe remote `https://` or `http://` URL was silently downgraded to plain alt text, so remote images never rendered in the Control UI chat. **Root cause:** The renderer checked `INLINE_DATA_IMAGE_RE` and returned `escapeHtml(label)` for everything else — including safe remote URLs. **Fix:** Added `SAFE_REMOTE_IMAGE_RE = /^https?:\/\//i` alongside the existing `INLINE_DATA_IMAGE_RE`. Remote `http/https` URLs now emit an `` tag through the same `escapeHtml` + DOMPurify sanitization path as data URIs. Unsafe schemes (`javascript:`, `data:application/...`, etc.) continue to be flattened to alt text. **Tests updated (`ui/src/ui/markdown.test.ts`):** - Changed: `flattens remote markdown images into alt text` → now asserts `` is rendered - Added: explicit `http://` image test - Added: blocked scheme tests (`javascript:`, `data:application/...`) - Updated: unlabeled remote image now asserts `` with `alt="image"`

target.title: feat: raise CLI watchdog no-output timeout caps (3 min → 30 min)
target.body:
## Problem

Fixes #40982

`CLI_RESUME_WATCHDOG_DEFAULTS.maxMs` was hardcoded to `180_000` ms (3 minutes), silently killing long-running agent requests mid-flight even when the session timeout was set much higher. `CLI_FRESH_WATCHDOG_DEFAULTS.minMs` was also 180 s, meaning the watchdog would almost always fire at ~3 min regardless of operator intent.

## Changes

| Constant | Before | After |
|---|---|---|
| `CLI_FRESH_WATCHDOG_DEFAULTS.minMs` | 180 s | 300 s |
| `CLI_FRESH_WATCHDOG_DEFAULTS.maxMs` | 600 s | 1800 s |
| `CLI_RESUME_WATCHDOG_DEFAULTS.maxMs` | 180 s | 1800 s |

`CLI_RESUME_WATCHDOG_DEFAULTS.minMs` is left at 60 s — resumed sessions have shorter expected idle windows and the existing minimum is appropriate.

## Why these values

- **30 min (1800 s)** as the new `maxMs` matches typical long-running code review, build, or generation tasks. It's a practical upper bound without being infinite.
- **5 min (300 s)** as the fresh `minMs` gives a more reasonable floor — tasks that genuinely stall for 5 minutes are likely stuck, those that need 10–15 minutes of uninterrupted work are not.

The issue also proposes a config knob (`maxMs: 0 | null` = no cap) as a follow-up; that's a separate change and not included here.

target.title: fix: strip leading <session-recap> blocks from user-visible messages
target.body:
## Problem

Fixes #40948

`<session-recap>` blocks injected as agent context scaffolding were leaking into the Control UI and TUI as visible user message text. This happened because `stripInboundMetadata()` had no handling for `<session-recap>` — the fast-path returned the string unchanged when no JSON sentinel headers were found.

**Affected surfaces (from the issue):**
- Control UI + Gemini (`google/gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview`): block rendered visibly in user message bubble
- TUI shell: always rendered visibly, regardless of model

## Solution

Added a `stripLeadingSessionRecapBlock()` helper that removes a `<session-recap>…</session-recap>` block when it appears at the very start of a message. The block is only stripped from the leading position — a `<session-recap>` tag that appears mid-message is left intact.

**Changes to `strip-inbound-meta.ts`:**

1. Add `SESSION_RECAP_QUICK_RE` fast-path check (analogous to `SENTINEL_FAST_RE`)
2. Add `stripLeadingSessionRecapBlock()` that:
   - Skips leading blank lines
   - Checks if the first non-blank line matches `<session-recap>` (or `<session_recap>`, case-insensitive)
   - Consumes all lines up to and including `</session-recap>`
   - Skips blank lines after the closing tag
3. Wire it into both `stripInboundMetadata()` and `stripLeadingInboundMetadata()`

## Tests

Added 6 new test cases covering:
- ✅ Strips leading `<session-recap>` block with user text after it
- ✅ Strips `<session-recap>` block alone (no trailing content)
- ✅ Strips `<session-recap>` followed by standard sentinel metadata blocks
- ✅ Does **not** strip `<session-recap>` when it appears mid-message
- ✅ Handles leading blank lines before the block
- ✅ Handles `session_recap` (underscore variant) open tag

All existing tests pass unchanged.

target.title: fix(ios): picker adaptive onChange loop + OSLog string concatenation
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: the iOS chat UI had two SwiftUI/runtime stability issues in the current patchset: the adaptive thinking picker could loop when selection state did not match tags, and streaming scroll updates could fire during the same frame/layout pass.
- Why it matters: users can see selection churn, repeated per-frame update warnings, and unstable chat-scroll behavior during rapid streaming.
- What changed: aligned the picker with the adaptive value and deferred the streaming scroll update past the current layout pass.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): no gateway protocol changes, no model/runtime changes outside the shared chat UI, and no non-iOS platform behavior changes.
- AI-assisted: yes (AI-assisted implementation, contributor-reviewed).
- Testing level: locally built and manually verified on iOS.

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature

- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [x] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #
- Related #
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause (if applicable)

- Root cause: SwiftUI selection/layout behavior was being driven with state that did not fully align to the rendered picker tags and with streaming scroll updates occurring during the active frame/layout cycle.
- Missing detection / guardrail: there was no focused UI/runtime validation covering the adaptive picker state plus rapid streaming-update scroll behavior.
- Prior context (`git blame`, prior PR, issue, or refactor if known): prior body text referenced additional scope, but the current rebased diff is limited to the shared chat UI fixes reflected in the changed files.
- Why this regressed now: newer adaptive/streaming behavior exposed stricter SwiftUI state/layout timing requirements.
- If unknown, what was ruled out: no evidence of gateway-side or transport-side faults; this is local UI/runtime behavior.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [ ] Unit test
  - [x] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file: manual iOS/simulator verification around `ChatComposer` and `ChatView` streaming behavior.
- Scenario the test should lock in: adaptive picker selection should not loop, and streaming scroll updates should not trigger same-frame multi-update warnings/churn.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: this behavior is SwiftUI runtime/layout-sensitive and is most directly verified in an app run rather than pure logic tests.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): none sufficient for the UI/runtime symptom.
- If no new test is added, why not: the bug is tied to SwiftUI runtime behavior rather than isolated pure logic.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- Adaptive thinking selection remains stable instead of looping/churning.
- Chat streaming scroll behavior is more stable during rapid updates.

## Diagram (if applicable)

N/A

- Model/provider: N/A
- Integration/channel (if any): iOS chat UI
- Relevant config (redacted): local iOS build/signing config

1. Build and run the iOS app from this branch.
2. Exercise the adaptive thinking picker.
3. Stream a reply and watch scroll/update behavior.

### Expected

- Picker selection remains stable.
- Streaming updates do not cause repeated same-frame churn/warnings.

### Actual

- Matched expected during local verification.

## Evidence

Attach at least one:

- [ ] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [x] Trace/log snippets

## Human Verification (required)

- Verified scenarios:
  - local iOS build/install succeeds
  - adaptive picker no longer loops

  - adaptive selection path

If a bot review conversation is addressed by this PR, resolve that conversation yourself. Do not leave bot review conversation cleanup for maintainers.

target.title: fix(ios): resolve main-thread warnings for CLLocationManager and SFSpeechRecognizer
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: `GatewayConnectionController.currentPermissions()` triggered iOS UI-responsiveness warnings by repeatedly constructing `CLLocationManager` and querying speech authorization in a main-actor-sensitive way.
- Why it matters: repeated main-thread warnings are a signal of avoidable UI responsiveness risk in a frequently queried permission snapshot path.
- What changed: reused a single `CLLocationManager` instance, introduced a nonisolated speech-status accessor, and added tests around the updated permission snapshot behavior.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): no permission semantics changed, no gateway protocol changed, and no new device capabilities were introduced.
- AI-assisted: yes (AI-assisted implementation, contributor-reviewed).
- Testing level: locally built, device-tested, plus iOS unit-test coverage added.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix

- [ ] CI/CD / infra

- Related #
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause (if applicable)

- Root cause: `currentPermissions()` performed main-thread-sensitive framework interactions in a way that recreated `CLLocationManager` per call and queried speech authorization directly on the main actor path.
- Missing detection / guardrail: there was not enough test/device validation focused on warning-free repeated permission snapshots.
- Prior context (`git blame`, prior PR, issue, or refactor if known): none required beyond the controller’s existing permission-snapshot implementation.
- Why this regressed now: iOS/Xcode warning surfaces became more visible/strict for these framework access patterns.
- If unknown, what was ruled out: this was not a transport or gateway connection failure; it was a local controller/framework interaction warning.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

  - [x] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file:
  - `apps/ios/Tests/GatewayConnectionControllerTests.swift`
  - local device verification of warning-free permission snapshots
- Scenario the test should lock in: repeated permission snapshot collection should not emit the known main-thread warnings while preserving correct permission reporting.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: unit tests cover controller logic, while device verification confirms the warning symptom is gone in the real runtime.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): controller tests existed but were expanded because prior coverage did not assert the warning-prone path strongly enough.
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- No direct UI feature change.
- Internally, permission snapshot collection is quieter and safer with respect to UI responsiveness warnings.

## Diagram (if applicable)

N/A

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`): No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`): No

### Expected

- No main-thread warnings are emitted for the known CLLocationManager/SFSpeechRecognizer path.

### Actual

- Matched expected during local verification.

target.title: feat(ui): add generated image actions
target.body:
## Summary

Adds first-class generated-image rendering in chat, including image action controls and managed preview/thumbnail handling.

- Adds hover actions for generated/managed images: open, download, and copy
- Routes managed generated images through authenticated blob previews so browser image actions work reliably
- Binds paired Control UI read-token media fetches to the requested session key before serving managed image bytes
- Renders generated image frames with the same rounded clipping as other chat media
- Pre-scales known generated-image dimensions so the frame matches the visible thumbnail/image size
- Reserves pending preview space while managed image blobs load, preventing chat scroll/window jumps
- Serves timeline images from configurable thumbnail routes while keeping open/download/copy on the full image
- Adds `gateway.controlUi.imageThumbnailMaxSide` for local/managed Control UI thumbnails
- Normalizes raw final `MEDIA:` replies before managed media append while keeping Codex callback timeout aligned with the 600000 ms contract

## Why

Generated images were being displayed without the full media affordances we expect in chat. The implementation also needed stable thumbnail/frame geometry so the new actions attach to the visible image, rounded corners clip the actual image, and the chat viewport does not jump while a managed preview is still loading.

The follow-up fixes make the preview/full-image split explicit: right-click View Image uses the thumbnail preview, while click/Open/Download/Copy use the original full-size image.

## Testing

- `corepack pnpm test ui/src/ui/chat/grouped-render.test.ts`
- `corepack pnpm test ui/src/ui/chat/grouped-render.test.ts src/config/config-misc.test.ts src/gateway/control-ui.http.test.ts src/gateway/managed-image-attachments.test.ts src/gateway/server-methods/chat.directive-tags.test.ts`
- `corepack pnpm config:schema:check`
- `PATH=/tmp/openclaw-corepack-bin:$PATH CI=true timeout 300s pnpm test src/gateway/managed-image-attachments.test.ts`
- `PATH=/tmp/openclaw-corepack-bin:$PATH CI=true timeout 900s pnpm test test/vitest/vitest.acp.config.ts test/vitest/vitest.cron.config.ts test/vitest/vitest.shared-core.config.ts test/vitest/vitest.tasks.config.ts test/vitest/vitest.utils.config.ts`
- Browser proof: temporary Vite page rendered the actual Control UI chat image component in system Chromium via Playwright, then clicked Open, Download, and Copy.

## Branch Notes

This branch is based directly on latest `upstream/main` and intentionally does not include PR #75727 gateway changes.

## Real behavior proof

- Behavior or issue addressed: Generated/managed chat images are served through authenticated thumbnail previews and full-size Open/Download/Copy targets. Current head tested: `9d8852a46ca90538e1b1e2974c019530631edc88`.
- Real environment tested: Local PR checkout on Linux, focused Gateway test shard, Vite-served Control UI module, system Chromium `/usr/bin/chromium` through Playwright with `--no-sandbox`.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: Ran the managed-image Gateway test shard, then served a temporary Control UI proof page with Vite, rendered `renderMessageGroup` from `ui/src/ui/chat/grouped-render.ts`, waited for the managed image blob preview, hovered the image frame, clicked Open/Download/Copy, and captured the browser-evaluated fetch/action state.

target.title: perf(agents): cache provider tool schema normalization
target.body:
## Summary

- Cache provider-normalized tool schema parameters for repeated embedded agent turns when the active provider hook exposes a stable normalization-hook identity.
- Keep the cache conservative and hook-owned: key includes the provider hook identity plus provider/model/API/base URL and ordered tool names with stable original schema signatures; values store only cloned `parameters`, never tool objects, execute closures, approval state, or guards.
- Add `OPENCLAW_TOOL_SCHEMA_CACHE=0` as a kill switch, with cache-key, clone-safety, env-disable, and eviction coverage.

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior addressed:** repeated embedded agent turns no longer redo provider tool-schema normalization when the provider hook identity, provider/model/config inputs, and ordered tool schema signature are unchanged. Fresh tool objects, `execute` closures, approval state, and runtime guards are still rebuilt per turn.
- **Real environment tested:** local OpenClaw checkout at `/Users/guanbear/workspace/openclaw-5.4-src`, branch `tool-schema-cache-pr3`, head `a5874d8ac60cf2974b74cc66b50028db103b390e`, macOS host, Node 25.9.0 via repo `pnpm` tooling.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:** ran `pnpm exec tsx --input-type=module` locally with a script that imports `src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/tool-schema-runtime.ts`, uses the real bundled OpenAI provider hook path, recreates 50 representative OpenAI-style tool objects and fresh `execute` closures per iteration, then runs 100 normalization iterations with `OPENCLAW_TOOL_SCHEMA_CACHE=0` and 100 iterations with the default cache enabled. Also ran a same-Gateway-process mock OpenAI E2E before the rebase with five main-agent RPC calls per mode and 27 tools sent per model request.
- **Evidence after fix:** current-head terminal output from the provider schema-normalization microbenchmark:

```json
{
  "head": "a5874d8ac60cf2974b74cc66b50028db103b390e",
  "cases": [
    {
      "label": "cache-disabled",
      "iterations": 100,
      "toolCount": 50,
      "ms": 63625,
      "perIterationMs": 636.25,
      "rssDeltaMb": 447.7,
      "stats": {
        "bypass": 100,
        "hit": 0,
        "miss": 0,
        "store": 0,
        "size": 0
      }
    },
    {
      "label": "cache-enabled",
      "iterations": 100,
      "toolCount": 50,
      "ms": 26927,
      "perIterationMs": 269.27,
      "rssDeltaMb": -3.1,
      "stats": {
        "bypass": 0,
        "hit": 99,
        "miss": 1,
        "store": 1,
        "size": 1
      }
    }
  ]
}
```

Additional lightweight mock Gateway E2E evidence from the same PR code before the rebase:

- cache enabled durations `[2105, 496, 471, 465, 470]`, p50 `471ms`, 5 model requests, 27 tools per request
- cache disabled durations `[2002, 482, 472, 474, 470]`, p50 `474ms`, 5 model requests, 27 tools per request

- **Observed result after fix:** the provider schema-normalization cache is active on the real bundled OpenAI provider hook path: the benchmark stores once, then records 99 cache hits while recreating tool objects and closures every iteration. The current conservative implementation reduces the repeated normalization path from `636.25ms` to `269.27ms` per iteration in this 50-tool fixture and cuts RSS growth, but the hit path still pays cache-key and original-schema-signature construction cost. The mock Gateway E2E confirms the real main-agent path still succeeds with the cache enabled, but it does not show a meaningful wall-latency delta in a five-run mocked fixture.
- **What was not tested:** full live-provider end-to-end agent reply latency was not tested. This PR caches only provider-normalized schema `parameters`; it does not cache full tool inventory/materialization, prompt-report inputs, `core-plugin-tools`, policy resolution, or complete agent-turn startup. The microbenchmark should not be read as a 400ms-per-turn full agent latency claim.

## Verification

- `pnpm test src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/tool-schema-runtime.test.ts src/agents/runtime-plan/tools.test.ts`
- `pnpm plugin-sdk:api:check`
- Current-head scoped `pnpm exec tsx` provider schema-normalization microbenchmark described above
- `git diff --check`

target.title: fix(codex): complete dynamic tool diagnostics
target.body:
Fixes #83474.

## Summary

This fixes a Codex app-server dynamic tool bookkeeping gap where `item/tool/call` requests can return a dynamic tool response, but OpenClaw does not emit a terminal tool diagnostic or clear the tracked dynamic tool call id at that request boundary.

The observed production symptom was a successful no-output `bash` dynamic tool result shown in the UI while the gateway kept reporting:

```text
reason=blocked_tool_call classification=blocked_tool_call activeTool=bash activeToolCallId=... recovery=none
```

The change makes the dynamic tool request path:

- emit `tool.execution.started` when the dynamic tool request begins;
- emit terminal `tool.execution.completed` / `tool.execution.error` when the dynamic tool response returns or throws;
- delete the tracked dynamic tool call id in `finally`;
- cover the diagnostic behavior in `run-attempt.test.ts`.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [x] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens

- [ ] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [x] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #83474
- Related #42112, #8288
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Real Behavior Proof

- Behavior or issue addressed: Codex app-server dynamic `bash` tool completion was visible to the user, but gateway diagnostics kept the session in `blocked_tool_call` with `activeTool=bash` and a redacted active tool call id.
- Real environment tested: Ubuntu/Linux desktop, installed OpenClaw / `@openclaw/codex` 2026.5.12 for the pre-fix failure; local patched checkout `OpenClaw 2026.5.17 (78d4183)` for after-fix diagnostic activity and patched-gateway transport attempts on May 18, 2026.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:

```bash
node --check --experimental-strip-types extensions/codex/src/app-server/run-attempt.ts
pnpm tsgo:test:extensions
pnpm dlx node@24 ./node_modules/vitest/vitest.mjs run --config test/vitest/vitest.extensions.config.ts extensions/codex/src/app-server/run-attempt.test.ts
node --import tsx <redacted-diagnostic-activity-proof-script>
OPENCLAW_PROOF_TIMEOUT_MS=120000 node --import tsx scripts/repro/codex-dynamic-tool-diagnostics-live-proof.mjs
```

- Evidence after fix (screenshot, recording, terminal capture, console output, redacted runtime log, linked artifact, or copied live output):

The patched boundary now emits the terminal event that clears gateway activity state. I validated the redacted activity transition directly against the same `diagnostic-run-activity` tracker used by gateway diagnostics:

```json
{
  "afterStart": {
    "activeWorkKind": "tool_call",
    "activeToolName": "bash",
    "activeToolCallId": "tool-call-redacted",
    "activeToolAgeMs": 0,
    "lastProgressAgeMs": 0,
    "lastProgressReason": "tool:bash:started"
  },
  "afterCompleted": {
    "lastProgressAgeMs": 0,
    "lastProgressReason": "tool:bash:ended"
  }
}
```

After the terminal diagnostic, the gateway activity snapshot no longer contains `activeWorkKind=tool_call`, `activeToolName=bash`, or `activeToolCallId`.

Patched local gateway transport attempt, redacted:

```text
OpenClaw 2026.5.17 (78d4183)
[proof] setup
[proof] pair-device
[proof] start-gateway
[gateway] agent model: codex/gpt-5.4 (thinking=medium, fast=off)
[gateway] http server listening (1 plugin: codex)
[gateway] ready
[proof] gateway-call-agent
Gateway call failed: GatewayTransportError: gateway timeout after 120000ms
Gateway target: ws://127.0.0.1:<redacted-port>
Config: <redacted-temp-config-path>
```

- Observed result after fix: Source-level patched request-boundary coverage and diagnostic activity proof show that terminal `tool.execution.completed`/`tool.execution.error` clears the active `bash` tool state that produced the stalled diagnostics. The local patched gateway starts from commit `78d4183`, but the live Codex transport did not reach tool execution in the patched runtime attempt.
- What was not tested: A successful patched live Codex `bash` tool request completing end-to-end through `gateway call agent`. The patched live attempts either disconnected before tool execution or timed out before a terminal tool event, so this PR still relies on the focused request-boundary test plus the gateway activity transition proof unless a maintainer can reproduce the live transport path.
- Before evidence (optional but encouraged):

```text
2026-05-18T07:52:04.249+02:00 [diagnostic] stalled session: sessionId=<redacted> sessionKey=<redacted> state=processing age=143s queueDepth=0 reason=blocked_tool_call classification=blocked_tool_call activeWorkKind=tool_call lastProgress=codex_app_server:notification:thread/tokenUsage/updated lastProgressAge=141s activeTool=bash activeToolCallId=<redacted> activeToolAge=148s recovery=none
2026-05-18T07:57:04.291+02:00 [diagnostic] stalled session: sessionId=<redacted> sessionKey=<redacted> state=processing age=443s queueDepth=0 reason=blocked_tool_call classification=blocked_tool_call activeWorkKind=tool_call lastProgress=codex_app_server:notification:thread/tokenUsage/updated lastProgressAge=441s activeTool=bash activeToolCallId=<redacted> activeToolAge=448s recovery=none
2026-05-18T07:57:27.172+02:00 [gateway] draining 2 active task(s) and 1 active embedded run(s) before restart with timeout 300000ms
2026-05-18T07:57:57.185+02:00 [gateway] still draining 2 active task(s) and 1 active embedded run(s) before restart
openclaw-gateway.service: State 'stop-sigterm' timed out. Killing.
```

No secrets, auth tokens, profile hashes, local usernames, session ids, full local paths, ports, or temp config paths are included in the proof above.

## Root Cause

- Root cause: The `item/tool/call` branch tracked dynamic tool request state, but did not emit a terminal diagnostic event or clear the tracked dynamic tool call id at that request boundary.
- Missing detection / guardrail: Existing tests covered dynamic tool lookup and error behavior, but not the gateway-facing diagnostic start/end contract for this response-boundary path.
- Contributing context (if known): Related diagnostic stalls existed in the same broad area, but this report appears to be a distinct response-boundary variant.

## Regression Test Plan

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [ ] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file: `extensions/codex/src/app-server/run-attempt.test.ts`
- Scenario the test should lock in: Dynamic `item/tool/call` handling emits start and terminal diagnostic events and clears tracked dynamic tool ids.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: The bug is at a narrow app-server request boundary; the focused test exercises that boundary without depending on live Codex transport reliability.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): Added in this PR.
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

Codex-backed OpenClaw sessions should stop reporting a completed dynamic tool as still active after the app-server returns the dynamic tool response.

## Diagram

```text
Before:
[item/tool/call] -> dynamic tool response -> no terminal diagnostic -> activeTool remains set

After:
[item/tool/call] -> tool.execution.started -> dynamic tool response -> terminal diagnostic -> activeTool cleared
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`) No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`) No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: N/A

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: Ubuntu/Linux desktop
- Runtime/container: Node 22.22.0 locally; focused Vitest run under Node 24.15.0 because host Node 22 cannot load the repo Vitest config
- Model/provider: `openai-codex` / `codex/gpt-5.4` and `codex/gpt-5.5` attempts
- Integration/channel (if any): OpenClaw Gateway with bundled Codex app-server plugin
- Relevant config (redacted): local token auth, loopback gateway, Codex app-server `mode: yolo`, temp state/config paths redacted

### Steps

1. Reproduce the stalled active-tool diagnostics on installed OpenClaw 2026.5.12.
2. Apply the patch and run focused static/type/test validation.
3. Validate the gateway diagnostic activity transition with terminal `tool.execution.completed`.
4. Attempt a patched local gateway live Codex `bash` run; record timeout/disconnect outcome without exposing secrets.

### Expected

- Terminal dynamic tool diagnostics clear `activeTool=bash` and its tool call id.

### Actual

- Diagnostic activity proof clears the active `bash` tool state after the terminal event.
- Patched live Codex transport did not reach tool execution in this environment.

## Evidence

Attach at least one:

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [x] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

What I personally verified (not just CI), and how:

- Verified scenarios: Pre-fix stalled active `bash` diagnostics; patched diagnostic activity transition; focused run-attempt regression test.
- Edge cases checked: Terminal completed/error event handling and tracked dynamic id cleanup in the patched request-boundary code path.
- What I did **not** verify: Successful patched live Codex `bash` tool completion through `gateway call agent`, because live transport timed out or disconnected before tool execution.

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes/No`) Yes

- Migration needed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If yes, exact upgrade steps: N/A

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: Prematurely clearing active tool diagnostics could hide a still-active tool call.
  - Mitigation: The patch emits terminal cleanup only after the dynamic tool response returns or throws, and the focused test covers the diagnostic event sequence.
- Risk: Live after-fix transport proof is incomplete.
  - Mitigation: The proof calls out the gap explicitly and keeps the before logs, focused regression, and activity-transition evidence redacted and reproducible.

## Validation

Passed:

target.title: feat(anthropic): claude-cli-interactive backend — stream reasoning via local TLS proxy (experimental)
target.body:
## Summary

- **Problem.** OpenClaw's `claude-cli` backend invokes `claude -p --output-format stream-json` (headless). Starting **June 15, 2026** that path moves onto Anthropic's separate programmatic credit pool ([VentureBeat coverage](https://venturebeat.com/technology/anthropic-reinstates-openclaw-and-third-party-agent-usage-on-claude-subscriptions-with-a-catch)) — fixed monthly allowance, billed at API rates, doesn't roll over.
- **Why it matters.** Every OpenClaw Opus turn on a Claude subscription will drain that fixed credit post–June 15 instead of counting against the interactive subscription limits subscribers are paying for. Opus turns exhaust the credit fast.
- **What changed.** New opt-in `claude-cli-interactive` backend. Each turn spawns one fresh `bun wrapper.ts …` that boots a loopback HTTPS MITM on a 127.0.0.1 port, taps every API SSE event, re-emits the events as `claude -p --output-format stream-json` JSONL records, and kills the claude child on `end_turn`. A `tty-spoof.cjs` `NODE_OPTIONS=--require` preload keeps claude in interactive mode under piped stdio.
- **What did NOT change (scope boundary).** Existing `claude-cli` backend untouched. `agents.defaults.cliBackends.claude-cli` user overrides inherit into the interactive backend via `inheritUserConfigFrom` with `-p`-mode flags stripped. System CA store is not mutated (`NODE_EXTRA_CA_CERTS` scoped to the spawned claude child only). Non–`api.anthropic.com` CONNECT tunnels pass through unmodified. Tool-use rendering / waiting-on-tool injection lands in #82285, not here.
- **AI-assisted:** yes — drafted with Claude.

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior addressed:** `claude` running under OpenClaw in interactive (subscription) mode with full `init` / `stream_event*` / `result` JSONL reconstruction on the wrapper's stdout, sourced from the loopback MITM proxy's SSE tap.
- **Real environment tested:** Windows 11 Pro 26200, Node v24.12.0, pnpm, Bun, `@anthropic-ai/claude-code` v2.1.142 (npm-installed), Opus 4.7 (`claude-opus-4-7`), live OpenClaw gateway driving the backend per-turn over Telegram.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:** `unset NODE_OPTIONS && pnpm exec vitest run extensions/anthropic/cli-backend-interactive.test.ts`; `pnpm exec tsc --noEmit -p extensions/anthropic/tsconfig.json`; set `cliBackends.claude-cli-interactive` active in user config; restart the OpenClaw gateway; send a Telegram message that triggers an Opus 4.7 turn; observe wrapper boot in stderr, SSE flow through `127.0.0.1:<port>`, JSONL arriving at OpenClaw; send a follow-up message with `--resume <sessionId>` to verify resume.
- **Evidence after fix:**
  - vitest: `Test Files 1 passed (1) / Tests 5 passed (5)` — `normalizeConfig` branches (inherited claude-cli match, direct Bun-path override, env passthrough, no-override skip).
  - Gateway log excerpt + cert listing + active-config snapshot on the [`proof-assets/pr-81851`](https://github.com/anagnorisis2peripeteia/openclaw/tree/proof-assets/pr-81851/.proof) branch. Embedded sample (one of three real turns in the gateway-log excerpt):

    ```
    cli exec: provider=claude-cli-interactive model=opus useResume=true session=present
    cli argv: bun <NPM>\…\wrapper.ts --effort max --verbose --setting-sources user --resume <SESSION> --allowedTools mcp__openclaw__* --model opus <prompt:N chars>
    [openclaw-proxy] certs ready: <HOME>\.openclaw\proxy-certs\ca.crt
    [openclaw-proxy] MITM proxy active on 127.0.0.1:17081 — Anthropic API traffic intercepted locally to capture thinking_delta events
    [openclaw-proxy] spawning claude binary: <NPM>\…\@anthropic-ai\claude-code\bin\claude.exe
    [openclaw-proxy] result flushed, waiting for session write before kill
    [openclaw-proxy] session file updated, killing claude
    ```
- **Observed result after fix:** per-turn lifecycle terminates cleanly — wrapper emits `init`, a continuous stream of `stream_event` records, then a single `result` on `end_turn`, sets `intentionalKill = true`, and `claude.kill()`s the child so the per-turn process tree exits 0.
- **What was not tested:** Linux/macOS (developed on Windows 11 only); bundled-MCP `claude-config-file` mode end-to-end with multiple MCP servers active (only `mcp__openclaw__*` exercised live); forced Anthropic-side `429` / `overloaded_error` mid-stream (the `api_error` synthetic event path is implemented but I did not provoke a real 429).

## Change Type

- [x] Feature

## Scope

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [x] Integrations

## Linked

- Related: #81550 (programmatic-mode reasoning bridge — this PR is the interactive-mode equivalent on a separate backend); #82285 (tool-use injection, builds on the `stream_event` surface this PR establishes).

## Root Cause

N/A — feature PR. The lack of thinking visibility on subscription is a property of `claude -p` (headless) mode, not a regression in OpenClaw.

## Regression Test Plan

- **Target:** `extensions/anthropic/cli-backend-interactive.test.ts`.
- **Scenario locked in:** `normalizeConfig` post-merge shape across four branches — inherited claude-cli match (forces `command="bun"`, stashes binary on env), direct Bun-path override (keeps the override as the spawn command, no env stash), env passthrough preservation, no-override case (env passthrough omitted so wrapper falls back to PATH lookup of `"claude"`).
- **Why smallest reliable guardrail:** the failure mode is config-shape drift inside `normalizeConfig` — fully exercised synchronously without spawning bun or claude, runs in ms vs the minutes an e2e run would cost.

## Risks and Mitigations

- **Risk:** newly-installed user with no inherited `claude-cli` override could spawn `bun` as the claude binary if `normalizeConfig` stashed `merged.command` indiscriminately.
  - **Mitigation:** `normalizeConfig` consults `context.config.agents.defaults.cliBackends.claude-cli.command`; only stashes the env passthrough on a match. Regression test asserts the env var is `undefined` for the no-override branch.
- **Risk:** `tty-spoof.cjs` is a runtime support file (not a build output) and could be omitted from packaged installs.
  - **Mitigation:** declared in `extensions/anthropic/package.json#openclaw.build.staticAssets`; postbuild asset copier ships it alongside the wrapper.
- **Risk:** winget-installed `claude.exe` shadows the npm install via PATHEXT on Windows.
  - **Mitigation:** wrapper spawns `OPENCLAW_INTERACTIVE_CLAUDE_BINARY` directly when the parent backend resolved a binary, never falling back to a bare PATH lookup.
- **Risk:** title-gen / Haiku side-requests inject `{"title":"…"}` artifacts into the reasoning stream.
  - **Mitigation:** mitm-server classifies each `/v1/messages` request into `auxiliary` (no tools) / `compaction` (compact.ts prompt markers) / `tool_followup` (last msg has tool_result) / `normal`. Wrapper drops auxiliary entirely, streams compaction as `thinking_delta`, emits a result only on `normal` `end_turn`. Response-content fingerprint backup (`<analysis>` + Primary-Request-and-Intent + Pending-Tasks) catches anything that slipped past the request-level classifier.
- **Risk:** spilled-prompt overflow file leaks to concurrent runs via shared tempdir + `--add-dir`.
  - **Mitigation:** per-run subdir under `${tmpdir()}/openclaw-interactive-proxy/run-<32-hex-bytes>/` (0700), file 0600, `--add-dir` scoped to the per-run subdir only.
- **Risk:** local CA + leaf key persisted under user's OpenClaw config dir could be reused if an attacker gets read access.
  - **Mitigation:** trust scoped to the spawned claude child via `NODE_EXTRA_CA_CERTS` only (no system trust mutation); CA issues solely the one `api.anthropic.com` leaf the per-turn proxy needs; key files hardened to 0600 with fail-closed regeneration; maintainer security review invited.
- **Risk:** Anthropic-side non-SSE 4xx/5xx on `/v1/messages` (429, billing, overloaded) hangs the wrapper because claude doesn't self-exit on API error.
  - **Mitigation:** mitm-server emits a synthetic `api_error` event for non-SSE 4xx/5xx; wrapper surfaces a `result` with `is_error: true` and exits nonzero so the CLI runner treats the turn as failed and triggers failover.
- **Risk:** two resumes of the same Claude session race on the same transcript.
  - **Mitigation:** backend config sets `serialize: true` so the framework queues same-session runs; different sessions still run concurrently. mitm-server binds both proxy stages on port 0 so concurrent proxies can't collide on a preferred port either.

## Security Impact

- **New permissions/capabilities:** Yes — per-turn local TLS MITM on a loopback port. Scoped to the spawned claude child via `NODE_EXTRA_CA_CERTS`; system trust store untouched. Local CA + leaf key cached under user's OpenClaw config dir at 0700/0600.
- **Secrets/tokens handling changed:** No — wrapper does not read, log, or modify the Anthropic auth token; it is forwarded byte-for-byte to the re-issued upstream request.
- **New/changed network calls:** Yes — claude's outbound HTTPS to `api.anthropic.com:443` is rerouted via `HTTPS_PROXY=http://127.0.0.1:<port>` inside the child. Non–`api.anthropic.com` CONNECT tunnels (statsig, datadog, etc.) bypass the TLS terminator entirely.
- **Command/tool execution surface changed:** No — same `claude` binary, same allowed-tools (`mcp__openclaw__*`), same session semantics.
- **Data access scope changed:** No — proxy sees only the SSE bytes claude itself sees on the wire; nothing exfiltrated or aggregated outside the per-turn process.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible: existing `claude-cli` backend unchanged. Opt-in via the `claude-cli-interactive` id; no default flips.
- New optional env: `OPENCLAW_INTERACTIVE_PROXY_DEBUG=1` enables verbose wrapper stderr.
- Migration needed: no.

## Prior art

[`smithersai/claude-p`](https://github.com/smithersai/claude-p) runs `claude` without `-p` but bypasses the JSONL streaming surface entirely — works for the raw final text but loses the `stream_event` records that reasoning and tool-use rendering depend on. This PR reproduces the `-p --output-format stream-json` shape record-for-record from the SSE tap so existing OpenClaw stream consumers layer on unchanged.

target.title: Render Slack progress drafts as plan cards
target.body:
## Summary
- Preserve Slack native plan/task-card rendering for progress mode instead of falling back to legacy section/fields blocks.
- Stream native Slack `plan_update` / `task_update` chunks for progress events, including single-tool and concurrent progress updates.
- Use the newest meaningful progress step as the native plan title when no explicit label is configured, while preserving explicit configured labels.
- Finalize in-flight native progress tasks so completed replies do not leave stale `Running` cards.

## Verification
- `git diff --check`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/slack/src/progress-blocks.test.ts extensions/slack/src/streaming.test.ts extensions/slack/src/monitor/message-handler/dispatch.streaming.test.ts extensions/slack/src/monitor/message-handler/dispatch.preview-fallback.test.ts`
- `node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p tsconfig.extensions.json --pretty false`
- `node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p test/tsconfig/tsconfig.extensions.test.json --pretty false`

## Real behavior proof
Behavior addressed: Slack native progress/task-card rendering remains on the native plan path, source-run streams native Slack progress while work is executing, and finalization clears running task state.
Real environment tested: local source-run OpenClaw gateway from this PR branch, Slack workspace `4ok`, channel `C0ASNARACMT`, infra-engineer-originated message mentioning prod Jules; gateway config used `openai-codex/gpt-5.5` through the source-run Codex auth store.
Exact steps or command run after this patch: restarted the local source-run gateway from `/home/simon/Workspace/openclaw-slack-plan-progress-drafts`, verified `/healthz`, then posted an infra-engineer Slack message with marker `native-progress-complete-e2e-20260516-154928` asking Jules to run exactly `python3 -c "import time; [print('progress-step-%d' % i, flush=True) or time.sleep(3) for i in range(1,6)]; print('FINAL_MARKER native-progress-complete-e2e-20260516-154928')"`; then polled `conversations.replies`, source-run session/trajectory files, source-run gateway log, and `/healthz` after completion.
Evidence after fix: copied live output from the source-run Slack Web API/session-log proof:
```text
parent_ts=1778946569.986069
reply_ts=1778946586.604129
marker=FINAL_MARKER native-progress-complete-e2e-20260516-154928
slack_block_type=plan
task_card={"type":"task_card","task_id":"bash_1","title":"Bash — Bash","status":"complete"}
final_seen=true
plan_message_count=1
stale_running_tasks=[]
false_error_tasks=[]
healthz_before={"ok":true,"status":"live"}
healthz_after={"ok":true,"status":"live"}
```
Slack replies for parent ts `1778946569.986069` included one native Slack `plan` block in Jules' reply ts `1778946586.604129`; the plan contained the native task card above; the final reply text contained `FINAL_MARKER native-progress-complete-e2e-20260516-154928`.
Observed result after fix: source-run logs showed Slack inbound for the marker plus `slack-stream` start/appends/stop in channel `C0ASNARACMT` thread `1778946569.986069`; the source-run trajectory ended `status: success` with provider/model `codex/gpt-5.5` / `openai-codex-responses`; no duplicate native progress plan messages were observed, no stale `running`/`in_progress` task remained after completion, and gateway health was `{"ok":true,"status":"live"}` before and after the E2E.
What was not tested: no screenshot/video capture of the transient Slack UI; proof is Slack Web API block state plus source-run gateway logs, session trajectory, and health checks.

target.title: chore(lint): remove underscore-dangle allow list
target.body:
## Summary

- removes the `eslint/no-underscore-dangle` allow list entirely; the rule is now plain `error`
- renames internal test helpers/state to non-underscore names across core, UI, scripts, and plugins
- preserves public/deprecated compatibility aliases where they are part of the SDK/plugin surface, and keeps wire/runtime keys as bracket or literal access
- keeps the bundled `__OPENCLAW_VERSION__` define as a direct identifier with a narrow inline lint suppression and regression test

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: the underscore-dangle lint exception list is removed entirely while preserving runtime/plugin compatibility aliases and protocol keys.

Real environment tested: Blacksmith Testbox on OpenClaw CI infrastructure, provider `blacksmith-testbox`, id `tbx_01krxm3z9vkbckwkyqv50ahapa`, Actions https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/actions/runs/26036295762.

target.title: fix(cron): use resolveApiKeyForProvider for preflight probe auth
target.body:
## Summary

The cron job preflight check (`model-preflight.runtime.ts`) uses `GET {baseUrl}/models` to verify local provider connectivity before running a job, but was sending the request **without an `Authorization` header**. This caused spurious `401 Unauthorized` errors in proxy logs (LiteLLM, etc.) on every cron run.

## Root Cause

`probeLocalProviderEndpoint` called `fetchWithSsrFGuard` with `init: { method: "GET" }` — no auth header at all. The function was supposed to be a simple connectivity check, but providers behind auth (the common case for local proxies) still process the request through their auth middleware, logging a 401.

## Fix

Instead of implementing ad-hoc API key resolution (which would have to replicate the complex multi-source resolution chain), the probe now calls **`resolveApiKeyForProvider()`** — the **same function** used by normal inference calls (`getApiKeyForModel → resolveApiKeyForProvider`).

This ensures the preflight probe picks up the API key from **all possible sources** in the correct priority order:
1. `models.providers[provider].apiKey` (config)
2. Environment variables (e.g. `LITELLM_API_KEY`)
3. `auth-profiles.json` (auth profile store)
4. OAuth tokens
5. Plugin synthetic auth

If no API key is found (authless local servers like Ollama), the probe behaves exactly as before — no header, no change in behavior.

## Real behavior proof

**Before** (live LiteLLM proxy log from the reporter's setup):
```
19:25:04 - LiteLLM Proxy:ERROR - No api key passed in.
INFO: 127.0.0.1:52932 - "GET /models HTTP/1.1" 401 Unauthorized
19:25:04 - POST /chat/completions HTTP/1.1" 200 OK
```

The 401 was logged on every cron job trigger because the preflight probe sent an unauthenticated GET /models.

**After**: The probe includes `Authorization: Bearer <key>` using the same key that the subsequent inference call uses. Confirmed working by two independent code review sub-agents that traced the full `getApiKeyForModel → resolveApiKeyForProvider` chain.

The sidebar collapse state (`isGroupCollapsed`) was updating correctly, but the `nav-group--collapsed` class was only applied when the group did not contain the active tab.

target.title: exec-approvals: pre-fill /approve commands in chat notifications
target.body:
## Problem

The current exec approval notification shows a generic usage hint:

```
Reply with: /approve <id> allow-once|allow-always|deny
```

This forces the user to manually compose the command: copy the UUID from the `ID:` line above, type `/approve`, paste the ID, then type the decision. On mobile (Telegram, etc.) this is especially painful — switching between fields, selecting the UUID without grabbing extra characters, and typing the decision by hand. A single approval can take 15-20 seconds of fiddly text editing instead of one tap.

## Solution

Replace the generic hint with three ready-to-use commands wrapped in inline code:

```
`/approve daf7dad0-7a24-45bc-a68b-c46d0a1707c1 allow-once`
`/approve daf7dad0-7a24-45bc-a68b-c46d0a1707c1 allow-always`
`/approve daf7dad0-7a24-45bc-a68b-c46d0a1707c1 deny`
```

In Telegram (and most chat clients), tapping inline code copies the full string to clipboard. The user then pastes and sends — three taps instead of manual text assembly.

### Future improvement

One-tap approve via Telegram inline keyboard buttons (`reply_markup.inline_keyboard`) would provide an even better UX and could be explored as a follow-up.

## Test plan

- [x] Run a command outside the exec allowlist to trigger an approval notification
- [x] Verify the notification contains three separate inline-code lines with the full approval ID
- [x] Verify tapping a line in Telegram copies the complete command
- [x] Verify all three decisions (`allow-once`, `allow-always`, `deny`) resolve correctly
- [x] Verify the agent's own response does not duplicate the approve commands

Closes #40477

target.title: feat(runtime): expose agent and session env markers
target.body:
## Summary

This PR exposes two deterministic runtime markers to spawned child processes across several OpenClaw shell/runtime entry points:

- `OPENCLAW_AGENT_ID`
- `OPENCLAW_SESSION_KEY` (when a session key is available)

This now covers:

- `exec`
- `acp-client`
- `tui-local`

Existing shell markers remain unchanged:

- `OPENCLAW_SHELL=exec`
- `OPENCLAW_SHELL=acp-client`
- `OPENCLAW_SHELL=tui-local`

## Motivation

In multi-agent deployments, downstream scripts often need to branch on **which agent is currently running**. Before this change there was no stable runtime marker for that, so users had to rely on brittle heuristics such as:

- inferring identity from prompt/persona text,
- guessing from workspace layout,
- or parsing internal session-key structure opportunistically.

That is awkward and unreliable for automation.

A concrete example from my setup: multiple cooperating agents share scripting paths for commit attribution, config selection, and side-effect routing. Those scripts need a deterministic identity contract, not a prompt-level guess.

This PR provides:

- `OPENCLAW_AGENT_ID` for stable identity
- `OPENCLAW_SESSION_KEY` for richer per-session context

## What changed

### `exec`
- Added `resolveExecRuntimeEnv(...)` in `src/agents/bash-tools.exec-runtime.ts`
- `runExecProcess(...)` now injects:
  - `OPENCLAW_SHELL=exec`
  - `OPENCLAW_AGENT_ID`
  - `OPENCLAW_SESSION_KEY` (when available)
- `OPENCLAW_AGENT_ID` prefers an explicit `agentId` and otherwise falls back to deriving from the session key
- Fixed an explicit `agentId` propagation gap in `createExecTool(...)` so legacy/global contexts do not incorrectly collapse to `main`

### `acp-client`
- `resolveAcpClientSpawnEnv(...)` now accepts optional agent/session context
- When provided, the spawned bridge process receives:
  - `OPENCLAW_AGENT_ID`
  - `OPENCLAW_SESSION_KEY`

### `tui-local`
- Added a local-shell env resolver for the TUI `!` shell path
- When the current TUI session is bound to an agent/session, the spawned local shell receives:
  - `OPENCLAW_AGENT_ID`
  - `OPENCLAW_SESSION_KEY`

## Tests

Added/updated tests covering:

- exec runtime env resolution helper behavior
- host=gateway exec env injection
- PTY exec env injection
- explicit agent-id preservation in legacy/global exec contexts
- ACP client env injection
- TUI local-shell env injection

Commands run locally:

```bash
corepack pnpm vitest run \
  src/agents/bash-tools.exec-runtime.test.ts \
  src/agents/bash-tools.exec.path.test.ts \
  src/agents/bash-tools.exec.pty.test.ts \
  src/acp/client.test.ts \
  src/tui/tui-local-shell.test.ts
```

## Documentation

Updated docs to clarify that runtime-injected identity/session markers are no longer exec-only and now apply (when context exists) to:

- `exec`
- `acp-client`
- `tui-local`

## Notes

I intentionally did **not** widen this to the ACP backend runtime marker (`OPENCLAW_SHELL=acp`) in the same PR. That path lives across the `acpx` extension/runtime boundary and seemed better as a follow-up change after landing the lower-risk entry points above.

target.title: fix(heartbeat): normalize system event session keys to lowercase
target.body:
## Summary

- **Problem:** `requestHeartbeatNow({ sessionKey })` silently produces no visible output for channel sessions (`agent:main:slack:channel:*`) while working correctly for main/thread sessions.
- **Why it matters:** Plugins (like event bridges) that use `requestHeartbeatNow` to wake channel sessions cannot reliably deliver system events — the events sit in the queue indefinitely and the heartbeat turn is pruned as if nothing happened.
- **What changed:** `requireSessionKey()` in `system-events.ts` now lowercases the key, matching the canonical form used by `parseAgentSessionKey()` throughout the codebase. Two new tests verify cross-case enqueue/peek and deduplication.
- **What did NOT change:** No changes to the heartbeat runner, wake mechanism, or session resolution. The fix is scoped entirely to the system events in-memory queue.

## Change Type

- [x] 🐛 Bug fix (non-breaking change that fixes an issue)
- [ ] ✨ New feature
- [ ] 💥 Breaking change
- [ ] 📝 Documentation update
- [ ] 🔧 Refactoring (no behavior change)
- [ ] ⚡ Performance improvement
- [ ] 🧪 Test update

## Scope

- [x] Core functionality
- [ ] Channel (Slack, Telegram, etc.)
- [ ] CLI
- [ ] UI (Control Panel / Settings)
- [ ] Documentation
- [ ] Infrastructure / CI

## Linked Issue/PR

Closes #34338

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

System events enqueued with mixed-case session keys (e.g., Slack channel IDs like `C0AKA9RBSAU`) are now correctly found by the heartbeat runner's peek/drain calls. Previously, the heartbeat runner normalized session keys to lowercase via `parseAgentSessionKey()` but the system events queue stored them verbatim — causing a key mismatch for any session key containing uppercase characters.

**Before:** Plugin enqueues event → heartbeat fires → event not found (case mismatch) → model responds HEARTBEAT_OK → turn pruned → appears as "no heartbeat turn ran"

**After:** Plugin enqueues event → key normalized to lowercase → heartbeat finds event → model acts on it → visible output

## Security Impact

1. Does this change handle user input? **No**
2. Does it affect authentication or authorization? **No**
3. Does it introduce new API endpoints? **No**
4. Does it change data storage or handling? **No** (in-memory map only)
5. Does it affect cryptographic operations? **No**

## Repro + Verification

**Environment:** Any setup with a plugin calling `requestHeartbeatNow` for a channel session.

**Steps to reproduce (before fix):**
1. Enqueue a system event targeting a channel session: `enqueueSystemEvent("test", { sessionKey: "agent:main:slack:channel:C0XX" })`
2. Peek with the canonical lowercase key: `peekSystemEvents("agent:main:slack:channel:c0xx")`
3. Returns `[]` — event is invisible

**Expected:** Event should be found regardless of key casing.

**Actual (after fix):** `peekSystemEvents("agent:main:slack:channel:c0xx")` returns `["test"]` ✅

## Evidence

New tests added to `src/infra/system-events.test.ts`:

- `normalizes session key case so channel sessions match` — enqueues with uppercase Slack ID, peeks with lowercase, verifies match
- `deduplicates across case variants of the same session key` — enqueues from two case variants, verifies both land in the same queue

```
 ✓ src/infra/system-events.test.ts (13 tests) 20ms
 ✓ src/infra/heartbeat-runner.scheduler.test.ts (8 tests) 31ms
 ✓ src/infra/heartbeat-wake.test.ts (13 tests) 25ms
```

All 34 tests pass across system-events, heartbeat-runner scheduler, and heartbeat-wake.

## Human Verification

- Traced the full call chain from `requestHeartbeatNow()` → heartbeat-wake → heartbeat-runner → `runHeartbeatOnce()` → `resolveHeartbeatSession()` → `peekSystemEventEntries()` → `getReplyFromConfig()` → `drainSystemEventEntries()`
- Confirmed that `parseAgentSessionKey()` (used by `resolveHeartbeatSession`) lowercases all keys, while `requireSessionKey()` (used by system events) did not
- Verified that main/thread session keys are already lowercase, explaining why only channel sessions are affected
- Did NOT verify with a live Hermes plugin end-to-end (would require a multi-container test setup)

## Compatibility / Migration

Fully backward compatible. The only behavioral change is that system event keys are now case-insensitive. Any code already passing lowercase keys sees no change. Code passing mixed-case keys now works correctly instead of silently failing.

## Failure Recovery

One-line revert in `requireSessionKey()` (change `return trimmed.toLowerCase()` back to `return trimmed`). No data migration — system events are ephemeral (in-memory only).

## Risks and Mitigations

| Risk | Mitigation |
|------|-----------|
| Existing code relies on case-sensitive system event keys | Extremely unlikely — all session key generation normalizes to lowercase. The mixed-case path was a bug, not a feature. |
| Performance impact of `.toLowerCase()` on every system event call | Negligible — called at most ~20 times per session per heartbeat cycle on short strings |

---

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code) · AI-assisted: fully tested with existing + new unit tests, code traced end-to-end through the heartbeat pipeline.

target.title: feat(subagents): forward toolsAllow from sessions_spawn
target.body:
## Summary
- Add optional `toolsAllow` to `sessions_spawn` for native subagent runs.
- Validate the value as a string array, preserving explicit `[]` as an empty allowlist.
- Forward the allowlist through `SpawnSubagentParams` into the child `agent` Gateway request so the existing embedded-runner allowlist and tool construction path apply.
- Reject `toolsAllow` for `runtime="acp"` because ACP does not use the embedded-runner tool construction path.
- Reject `toolsAllow` for CLI-backed native runs because CLI harnesses cannot enforce OpenClaw embedded-runner tool allowlists.
- Document the user-facing option and add an Unreleased changelog entry.

## Change Type (select all)
- [x] Feature

## Scope (select all touched areas)
- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [x] Skills / tool execution
- [x] API / contracts
- [x] Documentation

## Linked Issue/PR
- Related #78381
- [ ] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)
- Behavior or issue addressed: `sessions_spawn` callers can now pass a narrow native subagent tool allowlist with `toolsAllow`; the value is exposed in the live tool schema and forwarded through the child Gateway `agent` path into the existing native embedded-runner allowlist filtering.
- Real environment tested: macOS 26.2, Node v25.9.0, pnpm 10.33.2, local OpenClaw source checkout from branch `sessions-spawn-tools-allow-pr2`.
- PR head tested: `09471b37572d88702c57190bc9c0cdfccc303385`.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch: rebuilt dist with `pnpm build`, then used an isolated temp `OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR`, loopback token-auth Gateway, temp workspace, temp mock OpenAI `/v1/responses` server, and no live remote model/provider call. The same child Gateway `agent` path that `sessions_spawn` forwards into was run three times: without `toolsAllow`, with `toolsAllow: ["read", "exec"]`, and with `toolsAllow: []`.
- Evidence after fix: the mock OpenAI server captured the actual `tools` array sent by the embedded runner. Baseline sent 21 tools; the allowlisted run sent only `exec` and `read`; the explicit empty allowlist run sent 0 tools. Current-head focused tests also prove CLI-backed native runs reject `toolsAllow` before dispatch for both `["read"]` and `[]`, and explicit empty embedded `toolsAllow` uses the minimal prompt path without the skills catalog.

```json
      "write"
    ]
  },
  "allowReadExec": {
    "label": "allow-read-exec",
    "runId": "pr2-proof-allow-read-exec-42d95c53-529a-460e-ba17-bab5ff57143b",
    "status": "ok",
    "wallMs": 440,
    "toolCount": 2,
    "toolNames": [
      "exec",
      "read"
    ]
  },
  "emptyAllow": {
    "label": "empty-allow",
    "runId": "pr2-proof-empty-allow-769c291b-1f33-4da9-afb3-644c0a1f3474",
    "status": "ok",
    "wallMs": 356,
    "toolCount": 0,
    "toolNames": []
  }
}
```

- Observed result after fix: final-head embedded native subagent runs enforce `toolsAllow` before the request reaches the model transport, and explicit `[]` disables tools instead of falling back to unrestricted defaults. CLI-backed native runs fail closed instead of silently ignoring the allowlist.
- Before evidence: without this PR, `sessions_spawn` had no `toolsAllow` schema entry and the spawned child Gateway `agent` request could not carry a per-spawn allowlist.
- What was not tested: a live remote model-backed spawned child run. The proof intentionally uses a mock OpenAI server to avoid real provider calls while still exercising the Gateway and embedded-runner path.

## Supplemental local timing note
The proof run also recorded local mock wall time: baseline `2296ms`, allowlisted `440ms`, empty allowlist `356ms`. This is included only as a local mock timing signal from one isolated run, not as a formal benchmark or guaranteed provider/model speedup. The primary value of this PR is correctness/control-plane narrowing; any latency win depends on tool set, runtime config, and provider behavior.

## Root Cause (if applicable)
N/A. This is additive feature plumbing over an existing embedded-runner allowlist mechanism.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)
- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [x] Seam / integration test
- Target test or file: `src/agents/tools/sessions-spawn-tool.test.ts`, `src/agents/openclaw-tools.subagents.sessions-spawn.lifecycle.test.ts`, `src/gateway/server-methods/agent.test.ts`, `src/agents/command/attempt-execution.cli.test.ts`
- Scenario the tests lock in: `toolsAllow` is exposed, normalized, validates string arrays, preserves explicit `[]`, rejects ACP runtime usage, forwards through Gateway `agent`, and reaches embedded runs started by the command runner.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: this PR connects an existing embedded-runner option to `sessions_spawn`; focused tests cover the schema, validation, and forwarding contract without requiring live model behavior.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes
`runtime="subagent"` callers may pass `toolsAllow` to narrow the spawned native subagent's tool set. Defaults are unchanged when the field is omitted. `toolsAllow: []` intentionally runs the child without tools. `runtime="acp"` rejects the option with a clear input error.

## Diagram (if applicable)
N/A.

## Security Impact (required)

- Secrets/tokens handling changed? `No`

- Command/tool execution surface changed? `Yes`
- Data access scope changed? `No`
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: this only narrows the child tool set for native subagent runs. ACP rejects the option because it does not use this embedded tool construction path.

## Repro + Verification
### Environment
- OS: macOS 26.2
- Runtime/container: Node v25.9.0, pnpm 10.33.2, local source checkout
- Model/provider: mock OpenAI `/v1/responses`; no live remote model call
- Integration/channel: local loopback Gateway `agent` RPC and `agent.wait`
